title: Risk Assessment Framework
body: At ADW, the gap between a safe SOP and a dangerous shift is usually a RA that got rubber-stamped or never communicated to the crew. When the controls in the RA aren't physically in place before work starts, the approval signature is worthless.
keyPoints: The post-control risk ranking is the only number that matters — if residual risk is still High after controls are listed, the work doesn't start. That's an escalation, not a workaround.

As Load and Haul Superintendent, your signature on the RA approval chain means you've authorized the work to begin. That authority comes with accountability — verify the controls are in place, not just on paper.

RA controls feed directly into the daily pit inspection: dig limits at white tape, geotech condition, berm integrity, operator competency, spotter placement. If any of those aren't confirmed on the walkthrough, the RA is void in practice.

The FLRA (Field Level Risk Assessment) is the operator-level tool — bilingual, four steps: preparation, hazard ID, risk assessment, control sign-off. Operators should be completing this before non-routine tasks, not after.

Recurring control patterns across ADW's RA set: edge-distance restrictions, day-shift only for high-risk work, geotech inspection before start, and "effective supervision and effective radio communication" appear in nearly every single one.
apply: With the ISO IMS Surveillance Audit running today (ADW online Jun 30), auditors will likely pull active RAs and check whether controls are physically visible on site — make sure your Stage 4 and Stage 6 edge controls, dig limits, and spotter placements match what's written.
